commonMistakesSection.mistake: Forgetting to add TMG function group to the same transport as the table
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: QA/Prod imports table only and SM30 fails for users
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Bundle table, TMG function group, search helps, and lock FMs in one transport

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Directly modifying SAP standard tables instead of using append or CI_
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Upgrades overwrite changes losing custom fields
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use APPEND STRUCTURE (ZZ prefix) or CI_ customer include for upgrade-safe extension

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Skipping ST05 trace before creating secondary indexes
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Indexes added without evidence may not improve performance and add maintenance cost
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Run ST05 to capture slow SQL, analyze plan, then design targeted index

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Building Z config tables without lock objects when concurrent edits possible
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Two users editing same record may overwrite each other's changes
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Define EZ-prefixed lock object on key combination and enforce ENQUEUE in SM30 events

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Giving theoretical answers without project context as a fresher
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Interviewers identify lack of real-world depth
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use training cases or internships and explain decisions you made even on small examples

data-dictionary-057.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Real-time Data Dictionary work covers designing Z tables, extending standard tables, building search helps, and managing locks in projects.

Used for:
- Custom Z config and transactional tables with proper settings
- Standard table extension via append structures
- Custom F4 search helps and lock objects for concurrency

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- SE11 for tables, structures, lock objects; SE16 for inspection
- SM30 for SM34 maintenance; ST05 for performance trace; SM12 for lock monitoring
- ZZ prefix for append; EZ prefix for lock objects; Z for tables

Interview keyword: Bundle table, TMG, search help, and lock in one TR; use append for standard tables; verify with ST05/SM12.

followupAnswerBank.question: Can you walk me through how you designed a custom Z-table from scratch?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Requirements gathering: functional team se mil ke samjho kya data store karna hai, key fields kya hain, aur ye configuration hai ya transactional data. Table design decisions: client dependency ke liye MANDT pehli key field, business key se match karte hue meaningful key fields, appropriate domains aur data elements use karke data types — jahan available standard data elements (WERKS, MATNR) reuse karo aur jahan needed wahan custom create karo. Configuration ke liye delivery class C set karo, transactional ke liye A. Enhancement category set karo. SM30 access ke liye Table Maintenance Generator generate karo. Reference fields pe foreign keys define karo — example, T001W check table ke saath WERKS key field. Secondary index sirf tab create karo jab queries require karen aur testing benefit confirm kare.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Requirements gathering: meet functional team to understand what data needs to be stored, what the key fields are, and whether it is configuration or transactional data. Table design decisions: MANDT as first key field for client dependency, meaningful key fields matching business key, data types using appropriate domains and data elements — reuse standard data elements where available (WERKS, MATNR) and create custom ones where needed. Set delivery class C for configuration, A for transactional. Set enhancement category. Generate Table Maintenance Generator for SM30 access. Define foreign keys on reference fields — for example, WERKS key field with T001W as check table. Create secondary index only if queries require it and testing confirms benefit.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Built ZCFG_SHIFT_CONFIG for 3 plants, 15 shift combinations. Used existing domain WERKS_D for plant field — no custom domain needed. Created custom domain ZSHIFT_TYPE with fixed values E/L/N. Delivery class C — transport carried config data to production correctly. TMG generated — plant managers could maintain shifts via SM30 without developer involvement. Saved 5 ABAP change requests per month.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Custom data element aur domain banana jab existing standard ones kaam kar sakte hain. WERKS field ke liye WERKS_D data element already exists — reuse karo, recreate mat karo. Standard data element = standard F4, standard validation, standard label.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Full table design in SE11: ZCFG_SHIFT_CONFIG
" Delivery class: C (Customizing)
" Enhancement category: Can be enhanced (deep)

" Key Fields:
" MANDT  CLNT      3     X  Client
" WERKS  WERKS_D   4     X  Plant (FK → T001W)
" ZSHIFT ZSHIFT_D  1     X  Shift (domain: E/L/N)

" Data Fields:
" ZSTART TIMS      6        Start Time
" ZEND   TIMS      6        End Time
" ZCAP   DEC    13,3        Capacity (tons)
" ZACTV  CHAR      1        Active Flag

" After creation: SM30 maintenance via TMG
" Transport: Table + Domain + Data Elements together

followupAnswerBank.question: How did you create a custom search help in a real project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek elementary search help ZSRH_MAT_PLANT create kiya. Selection method MARC (plant-material combination table) tha kyunki humein plant-specific material lists chahiye the. Import parameter WERKS calling screen context se automatically pass hota tha. Search fields MATNR aur MAKTX the jisse users material number ya description se search kar sake. Return field MATNR selection pe calling screen field ko fill karta tha. Search help ek custom data element ZMATNR_PLANT se attach tha. Is data element ko use karne wala koi bhi field automatically plant-filtered F4 get karta tha — ek baar create kiya, jahan jahan data element use hota hai wahan kaam karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Created an elementary search help ZSRH_MAT_PLANT. Selection method was MARC (plant-material combination table) since we needed plant-specific material lists. Import parameter WERKS was passed automatically from the calling screen context. Search fields were MATNR and MAKTX allowing users to search by material number or description. Return field MATNR filled the calling screen field on selection. The search help was attached to a custom data element ZMATNR_PLANT. Any field using this data element automatically gets the plant-filtered F4 — created once, works everywhere the data element is used.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Impact: Before custom search help, users typed material numbers manually — 20-30% entry errors due to wrong material numbers. After search help deployment, errors dropped to near zero. Business users very satisfied — they could search by material description instead of memorizing numbers. One data element change → 8 custom screens all got correct F4 automatically.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Search help banake data element mein attach karna bhool jaana. Common scenario: developer creates search help, calls it done, but F4 still shows generic list. Root cause: missing assignment in data element → Search Help tab. Always verify F4 behavior after assignment.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11: Elementary Search Help ZSRH_MAT_PLANT
" Selection Method: MARC
" Dialog Type: Dialog with value restriction

" Search Help Parameters:
" WERKS  IMP  -  Plant (passed from screen)
" MATNR  EXP  X  Material (return field, search)
" MAKTX  EXP  -  Description (search only, display)

" Attach in SE11 Data Element ZMATNR_PLANT:
" → Further Characteristics → Search Help: ZSRH_MAT_PLANT

" Test: custom screen with ZMATNR_PLANT field
" F4 → popup shows plant-filtered materials only
" Search by description → works automatically

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle extending a standard SAP table with custom fields?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Standard table extension append structures ke through hoti hai — SAP standard table ko directly modify karke kabhi nahi. SE11 mein, standard table open karo aur Append Structure button click karo. Z-prefixed name (e.g., VBAK ke liye ZZ1_VBAK) ke saath append create karo. SAP field additions ke saath future conflicts avoid karne ke liye ZZ prefix wale custom fields add karo. Append ko activate karo — SE14 database conversion physical column ko database table mein add karta hai. Custom fields ab VBAK se SELECT karne wale sab ABAP programs mein available hain. Append structures SAP upgrades survive karte hain — standard table modification imports ke during preserve hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Standard table extension is done via append structures — never by modifying the SAP standard table directly. In SE11, open the standard table and click the Append Structure button. Create the append with a Z-prefixed name (e.g., ZZ1_VBAK for VBAK). Add custom fields with ZZ prefix to avoid future conflicts with SAP field additions. Activate the append — SE14 database conversion adds the physical column to the database table. The custom fields are now available in all ABAP programs selecting from VBAK. Append structures survive SAP upgrades — they are preserved during standard table modification imports.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Extended 4 standard tables in one project: VBAK (ZZEXT_SO_REF for external SO reference), VBAP (ZZEXT_LINE_REF for line reference), LFA1 (ZZVENDOR_CATEGORY), MARA (ZZMAT_ORIGIN). All activated successfully. After S/4HANA upgrade, all 4 appends were intact, programs worked without modification. Proved upgrade-safety of append structure approach.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Custom field naam ZZ prefix ke bina dena. Future SAP enhancement pack can add a field with same name — conflict, activation failure. Always ZZ or ZZ1_ prefix for append fields. Non-negotiable rule.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11: Append Structure ZZ1_VBAK
" Assigned to table: VBAK
" Fields:
" ZZEXT_SO_REF  CHAR  30  External SO Reference
" ZZINT_STATUS  CHAR   1  Integration Status

" After activation, use in ABAP programs:
SELECT vbeln, kunnr, zzext_so_ref, zzint_status
  FROM vbak
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders)
  WHERE zzext_so_ref IS NOT INITIAL.

" Update custom field on standard table:
UPDATE vbak SET zzext_so_ref = @lv_ext_ref
  WHERE vbeln = @lv_vbeln.

followupAnswerBank.question: How did you identify and fix a performance problem related to Data Dictionary?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Performance investigation process: pehle ST05 SQL trace use karke slow query identify karo — trace activate karo, slow report run karo, deactivate karo, trace display karo. Long execution time wali table dhundo aur us query pe EXPLAIN run karo. EXPLAIN Full Table Scan ya Full Column Scan dikhata hai bina index usage ke. Query jin WHERE clause fields use karti hai unhe check karo. SE11 mein jaao, table open karo, existing secondary indexes check karo — agar WHERE clause fields ke liye koi matching index nahi hai, ek create karo. Index activate karo, ST05 dobara run karo, verify karo ki EXPLAIN ab index usage dikhaata hai. Before aur after execution times measure karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Performance investigation process: first identify the slow query using ST05 SQL trace — activate trace, run the slow report, deactivate, display trace. Find the table with long execution time and run EXPLAIN on that query. EXPLAIN shows Full Table Scan or Full Column Scan without index usage. Check the WHERE clause fields the query uses. Go to SE11, open the table, check existing secondary indexes — if no matching index exists for the WHERE clause fields, create one. Activate the index, run ST05 again, verify EXPLAIN now shows index usage. Measure before and after execution times.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Fixed report ZRPT_ORDERS_DAILY running 45 seconds. ST05: query on ZCUSTOM_ORDERS with WHERE BUKRS ERDAT ZSTATUS — full scan, 800,000 rows. Created index ZCI001 on (BUKRS, ERDAT, ZSTATUS). After index: same query, 0.8 seconds. Users noticed immediately. This was ticket resolved within half a day — high business impact, fast resolution.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Index banana without verifying it will actually be used. On HANA S/4HANA, column store scan can be faster than index for some queries. Always verify with ST05 EXPLAIN after creating index. Do not assume index = faster.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Step 1: ST05 trace identifies slow query
" Step 2: EXPLAIN shows no index used

" Step 3: SE11 → ZCUSTOM_ORDERS → Indexes tab
" Create index ZCI001:
" Fields: BUKRS (client), ERDAT (date), ZSTATUS (status)
" Activate index

" Step 4: Verify index usage (ST05 EXPLAIN after index):
SELECT vbeln, matnr, zstatus
  FROM zcustom_orders
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders)
  WHERE bukrs   = @lv_bukrs
    AND erdat   BETWEEN @lv_from AND @lv_to
    AND zstatus = @lv_status.
" ST05 EXPLAIN: should now show Index Range Scan on ZCI001

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the CI_ include pattern and when do you use it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: CI_ includes customer include structures hain jo SAP explicitly standard tables mein extension points ke roop mein provide karta hai. Ye standard table definitions mein include components ke roop mein appear karte hain — example, BKPF (FI document header) table mein CI_BKPF. Custom fields add karne ke liye, SE11 mein CI_ include open karo aur wahan fields add karo — append structure ki zaroorat nahi. Advantage ye hai ki CI_ includes us table ke liye SAP ka sanctioned extension mechanism hain — ye upgrades survive karna guaranteed hain aur SAP ka apna enhancement cycle inhe consider karta hai. CI_ includes use karo jab ye exist karte hain; append structures use karo jab CI_ includes available nahi hain ya un tables ke liye jo CI_ includes nahi rakhti.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: CI_ includes are customer include structures that SAP explicitly provides in standard tables as extension points. They appear as include components in standard table definitions — for example, CI_BKPF in the BKPF (FI document header) table. To add custom fields, open the CI_ include in SE11 and add fields there — no append structure needed. The advantage is that CI_ includes are SAP's sanctioned extension mechanism for that table — they are guaranteed to survive upgrades and SAP's own enhancement cycle considers them. Use CI_ includes when they exist; use append structures when CI_ includes are not available or for tables that do not have them.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: FI document had to store an external payment reference. Instead of append on BKPF, found CI_BKPF already in the table structure. Added ZZPAYREF field (CHAR 20) to CI_BKPF. Field immediately available in BKPF. No SE14 conversion needed — CI_ include already part of BKPF structure. Cleaner than append for tables with CI_ includes.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Append structure banana jab CI_ include already exist karta hai. SAP ke tables mein pehle check karo: kya CI_ include available hai? If yes, prefer CI_ include over append — it is the SAP-approved extension mechanism for that specific table.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check if CI_ include exists in a standard table:
SELECT tabname, fieldname, rollname
  FROM dd03l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_fields)
  WHERE tabname   = 'BKPF'
    AND rollname LIKE 'CI_%'
    AND as4local = 'A'.
" If CI_BKPF appears → use it for custom fields

" SE11: Open CI_BKPF (structure)
" Add custom field: ZZPAYREF CHAR 20
" Activate CI_BKPF → automatically reflected in BKPF

" Use in ABAP:
SELECT belnr, bukrs, gjahr, zzpayref
  FROM bkpf
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_bkpf)
  WHERE zzpayref IS NOT INITIAL.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you explain your Data Dictionary experience if you are a fresher with no real project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Freshers ke liye, honest raho lekin training ya practice project ke through practical knowledge demonstrate karo. Ek self-built scenario describe karo: 'Apne ABAP training project mein, maine ek leave management system design kiya. Maine ZEMP_LEAVE table create ki MANDT, employee number PERNR, leave type, from-date, to-date, aur status ke saath. Maine leave types ke liye fixed values wala domain create kiya, proper labels ke saath ek data element, aur leave type selection ke liye ek search help. SM30 access ke liye Table Maintenance Generator generate kiya aur table read aur update karne ke liye ABAP programs likhe.' Dikhao ki tum concepts samajhte ho client project ke bina bhi — aur agar tumne SE11 hands-on practice kiya, ise explicitly bolo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For freshers, be honest but demonstrate practical knowledge through a training or practice project. Describe a self-built scenario: 'In my ABAP training project, I designed a leave management system. I created table ZEMP_LEAVE with MANDT, employee number PERNR, leave type, from-date, to-date, and status. I created a domain for leave types with fixed values, a data element with proper labels, and a search help for leave type selection. I generated a Table Maintenance Generator for SM30 access and wrote ABAP programs to read and update the table.' Show you understand the concepts even without a client project — and if you practiced SE11 hands-on, say so explicitly.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Interview coaching note: Many freshers say 'I know SE11 theoretically.' Better approach: even a 2-week self-practice in a free SAP system (SAP trial or training system) can give you hands-on to talk about. Build a simple Z-table, domain, data element, search help, lock object — you will have specific things to say. 'I created and tested...' is much stronger than 'I know about...'
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Fresher hoke bhi real project hone ka claim karna without specifics. Interviewer specific question poochh ke pakad leta hai. Better: honest raho, practice project mention karo, knowledge depth dikhao. Honesty + knowledge depth = positive impression.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Training project table: ZEMP_LEAVE
" MANDT  CLNT   3    KEY  Client

followupAnswerBank.question: What questions does an interviewer usually ask to verify real Data Dictionary experience?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Real experience verify karne wale interviewers specifics puchte hain: Apni custom tables pe kaunsi delivery class set ki aur kyun? Table Maintenance Generator mein kaunsa authorization group assign kiya? Append structure field names ke liye kaunsa prefix use kiya aur kyun? Kab tumhe SE11 change ke baad SE14 run karna pada? Tumne kaise dhunda ki data element change karne se kaunse programs affected the? Kya DDIC objects ke saath koi transport issues encounter hue — kya hua? In questions ko precisely answer karne ke liye actual hands-on experience chahiye. Har ek ke liye specific answers prepare karo: config ke liye delivery class C, appends ke liye ZZ prefix, structural changes ke baad SE14, impact ke liye WHERE-USED, SE01 ke through transport completeness.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Interviewers who want to verify real experience ask specifics: What delivery class did you set on your custom tables and why? What authorization group did you assign in the Table Maintenance Generator? What prefix did you use for append structure field names and why? When did you need to run SE14 after a SE11 change? How did you find which programs were affected when you changed a data element? Did you encounter any transport issues with DDIC objects — what happened? These questions require actual hands-on experience to answer precisely. Prepare specific answers for each: delivery class C for config, ZZ prefix for appends, SE14 after structural changes, WHERE-USED for impact, transport completeness via SE01.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real interview: 'Aapne kaunse lock objects use kiye aur FOREIGN_LOCK kaise handle kiya?' Candidate who practiced: 'ENQUEUE exception check kiya, SY-MSGV1 se lock holder name nikala, display mode set kiya.' Candidate who only read theory: 'FOREIGN_LOCK exception handle karna chahiye.' Big difference in specificity — first candidate got selected.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Interview mein vague answers dena — 'tables create kiye, domains banaye.' Specific bolo: 'ZCFG_PLANT table, delivery class C, MANDT + WERKS as keys, TMG with authorization group ZCFG.' Numbers aur specifics credibility establish karte hain.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle transport of Data Dictionary objects and their dependencies?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: DDIC transport ke liye sab dependent objects same transport mein ya correctly sequenced separate transports mein hone chahiye. Ek table apne domains aur data elements pe depend karti hai — agar domain target system mein missing hai, table activation fail hoti hai. TMG-generated programs (function group, function modules, screens) bhi transport mein hone chahiye. Verification process: SE01, transport request open karo, Object List tab mein sab DDIC object types check karo: TABD (table), DOMA (domain), DTEL (data element), FUGR (function group), DYNP (Dynpro screens). QA mein import ke baad, production mein release karne se pehle target system mein SE11 activation succeeds ye verify karo. Kabhi assume mat karo ki dependencies already target mein hain — hamesha explicitly verify karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: DDIC transport requires all dependent objects in the same transport or in correctly sequenced separate transports. A table depends on its domains and data elements — if domain is missing in target system, table activation fails. TMG-generated programs (function group, function modules, screens) must also be in the transport. Verification process: SE01, open transport request, check Object List tab for all DDIC object types: TABD (table), DOMA (domain), DTEL (data element), FUGR (function group), DYNP (Dynpro screens). After import in QA, verify SE11 activation succeeds in target system before releasing to production. Never assume dependencies are already in target — always verify explicitly.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Pre-production checklist used: open SE01 → list all objects in transport → compare against expected list (table, domain, data element, TMG programs). Found domain ZCFG_TYPE was in a separate transport from 2 weeks ago that was already released but not yet imported in production. Added domain explicitly to current transport — avoided activation failure in production.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Wo object pehle se target mein hoga' assume karna. Always check target system SE11 for each dependency. One missing domain = entire table activation fails = emergency at go-live.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Transport object types for a complete DDIC change:
" TABD → Table definition
" DOMA → Domain
" DTEL → Data element
" FUGR → Function group (TMG)
" DYNP → Screen (TMG)
" PROG → Program (if any test reports)

" Check if domain exists in target (verify before transport):
" In target system SE11:
SELECT domname, as4local
  FROM dd01l
  WHERE domname = 'ZCUSTOM_DOMAIN'
    AND as4local = 'A'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt).
" Empty = domain missing → add to transport

data-dictionary-058.easyUnderstanding:
Production incident for Data Dictionary — ye ek high-stress situation hoti hai jab SAP system ke tables ya structures mein koi problem aata hai live environment mein.

Sabse common production Data Dictionary incident hai: 'Table activate nahi ho rahi' ya 'Transport se table properly import nahi hua.'

Sochho ek real scenario: Saturday subah production mein ek transport import hua jisme ek custom table ZCUSTOM_ORDERS mein ek naya field add kiya gaya tha. Import ke baad, sab users ka ek specific transaction DUMP de raha tha — 'GETWA_NOT_ASSIGNED.' SAP team ke paas production access hai lekin kuch changes allowed nahi hote bina approval ke.

Step 1: SM21 check karo — system log mein error dekho.
Step 2: ST22 check karo — ABAP dump analyse karo.
Step 3: SE11 mein ZCUSTOM_ORDERS open karo — activation status check karo.
Step 4: SE14 mein 'Check' karo — database aur DDIC mein discrepancy hai?
Step 5: SE14 'Activate and Adjust Database' run karo — database table physically update karo.
Step 6: Transaction test karo — dump gaya?
Step 7: Verify SE16N mein data intact hai.

Kabhi kabhi incident domain ya data element missing se hota hai — tab transport dependency check karo aur missing object manually transport karo.

Hamesha rollback plan rakho — agar fix kaam nahi kiya toh kya karein.
data-dictionary-058.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Production Data Dictionary incidents ko types mein classify karo:

**Type 1: Table Activation Failure in Production**
- Symptom: transport import ke baad table inactive state mein
- Detection: SE11 mein table open karo — orange light = inactive
- Cause: dependent object (domain, data element) missing in transport
- Fix: identify missing object, transport it, activate table in SE11
- Emergency option: SE11 → Activate → if dependencies missing → import missing transport

**Type 2: SE14 Conversion Failure**
- Symptom: field added to DDIC but not physically in database
- Detection: SE14 Check shows discrepancy; programs dump on new field
- Cause: SE14 was not run after transport, or ran with error
- Fix: SE14 → Activate and Adjust Database → check log for success
- Risk: large tables → conversion may take time → plan maintenance window

**Type 3: Program Dumps After DDIC Change**
- Symptom: ABAP dumps (ST22) after a DDIC-related transport
- Cause: internal table in program has old structure, new field added to DB table
- Detection: ST22 dump analysis → ASSIGN/GETWA_NOT_ASSIGNED or TYPE CONFLICT
- Fix: activate table in SE11, check if SE14 needed, transport program fix

**Type 4: Lock-Related Production Incidents**
- Symptom: records stuck locked, users cannot edit
- Detection: SM12 shows stale lock entries (old timestamp, user offline)
- Fix: SM12 → delete stale lock entries → records accessible again
- Prevention: always DEQUEUE in FINALLY block in programs

**Type 5: Domain/Fixed Value Production Issue**
- Symptom: new status value entered, validation fails
- Cause: domain fixed value not added or transport not imported
- Fix: SE11 → domain → add fixed value → activate → transport urgently

**Incident Response Checklist:**
1. SM21: system log — when did issue start, what error messages
2. ST22: ABAP dumps — what is the dump reason
3. SE11: check activation status of affected table/object
4. SE14: check DB vs DDIC discrepancy
5. SE01/STMS: check if transport imported correctly, all objects
6. SM12: check for stale locks if user access issue
7. Fix → test → verify SE16N data intact
8. Document incident: root cause, fix, prevention

**Communication Protocol:**
- Immediately inform team lead and project manager
- Status update every 30 minutes
- Never make unauthorized production changes
- Change management approval even for emergency fixes
data-dictionary-058.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'I have handled Data Dictionary production incidents using a structured approach. The most common scenario is a table activation failure after a transport import. My first step is always to check SM21 system log and ST22 for ABAP dumps — these tell me when the issue started and what the exact error is.

For a table not activating in production, I go to SE11, open the table, and check the activation status — orange light means inactive. I run SE14 Check to see if there is a discrepancy between the DDIC definition and the physical database. If the database is missing a new column, I run SE14 Activate and Adjust Database — but only after checking data impact and getting proper approval.

If the issue is a missing dependent object — a domain or data element not in the transport — I identify the missing object, create an emergency transport, import it in the correct sequence, and then activate the main table.

For stale lock incidents where users cannot access records, I use SM12 to identify and delete stale lock entries after verifying the locking user is no longer active.

In all cases, I verify data integrity via SE16N after the fix and document the root cause and prevention steps.'

Hinglish tip: Incident response mein steps clearly bolo — SM21, ST22, SE11, SE14, SM12 — ye transactions ka naam lena shows hands-on knowledge. Vague 'maine issue fix kiya' nahi — specific tools aur steps.
data-dictionary-058.codeExamples:
" Production incident workflow in ABAP/transactions

" Step 1: Check system log (SM21 equivalent in ABAP)
CALL FUNCTION 'SLGD_LOG_READ'
  EXPORTING
    from_date  = sy-datum - 1
    from_time  = '000000'
  TABLES
    messages   = DATA(lt_syslog).

" Step 2: Check ABAP dump list (ST22 analysis)
SELECT mandt, username, datum, uzeit, repid, errty
  FROM snap
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_dumps)
  WHERE datum = @sy-datum
    AND errty = 'GETWA_NOT_ASSIGNED'
  ORDER BY datum DESCENDING, uzeit DESCENDING.

" Step 3: Check table activation status
SELECT tabname, as4local
  FROM dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_tables)
  WHERE tabname  = 'ZCUSTOM_ORDERS'
    AND as4local <> 'A'.  " Not Active = problem!

" Step 4: After SE14 fix — verify data intact
SELECT COUNT( * ) AS records
  FROM zcustom_orders
  INTO @DATA(lv_cnt).
WRITE: / 'Records after fix:', lv_cnt.
" Compare with pre-fix count to confirm no data loss
data-dictionary-058.interviewScriptHinglish: Production Data Dictionary incidents ek structured workflow ke saath handle hote hain — pehle SM21 system log aur ST22 dump analysis se symptom confirm karo, phir SE11 aur DD02L mein table activation status verify karo. Transport ke baad activation failures ke liye, SE14 Database Utility Adjust and Activate ek coordinated downtime window ke andar run karo. Stale lock incidents SM12 mein inspect kiye jaate hain, owner se contact kiya jaata hai, aur locks proper approval ke saath clear hote hain. Saath mein stakeholders ke saath clear communication, change management compliance, aur post-incident review essential hain. Tools used mein SM21, ST22, ST05, SM12, SE14, SE11, aur STMS shaamil hain.
data-dictionary-058.interviewScriptEnglish: Production Data Dictionary incidents are handled with a structured workflow — start with SM21 system log and ST22 dump analysis to confirm the symptom, then verify table activation status in SE11 and DD02L. For activation failures after transport, run SE14 Database Utility Adjust and Activate inside a coordinated downtime window. Stale lock incidents are inspected in SM12, the owner is contacted, and locks are cleared with proper approval. Throughout, clear communication with stakeholders, change management compliance, and a post-incident review are essential. Tools used include SM21, ST22, ST05, SM12, SE14, SE11, and STMS.

data-dictionary-058.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Production Data Dictionary incidents are structured workflows handling table activation failures, stale locks, and transport import issues in live systems.

Used for:
- Diagnosing and recovering from table activation or import failures
- Clearing stale enqueue locks blocking business processes
- Coordinating downtime windows and post-incident reviews

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- SM21 system log; ST22 dumps; SM12 locks; SE14 DB utility
- DD02L for activation status; SNAP for dump patterns; STMS for transports
- SE11 Where-Used; ST05 for performance issues

Interview keyword: SM21+ST22 first, SE14 in downtime, SM12 with owner approval, post-incident review mandatory.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the first thing you do when a production Data Dictionary incident is reported?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Pehla step accurate information gather karna hai: kaunsi transaction ya program fail ho rahi hai, kab se, kitne users affected hain, aur recently kya change hua — specifically kya transports import hue. Phir failure timestamp ke around error messages ke liye SM21 system log check karo. Recent ABAP dumps ke liye ST22 check karo — dump reason aur program name directly root cause point karte hain. Sirf root cause samajhne ke baad hi koi fix attempt hona chahiye. Diagnosis ke bina fix karne ki rush often issue ko worsen karti hai — example, large table pe data impact check kiye bina SE14 run karna production outage cause kar sakta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The first step is to gather accurate information: which transaction or program is failing, since when, how many users are affected, and what changed recently — specifically what transports were imported. Then check SM21 system log for error messages around the failure timestamp. Check ST22 for recent ABAP dumps — the dump reason and program name point directly to the root cause. Only after understanding the root cause should any fix be attempted. Rushing to fix without diagnosis often worsens the issue — for example, running SE14 on a large table without checking data impact can cause a production outage.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Incident: Users reported transaction ZCUSTOM_ORDER_MAINT failing with dump. First action: ST22 → dump 'GETWA_NOT_ASSIGNED' in program ZCUSTOM_MAINT → field ZNEW_FIELD in internal table not matching DB table structure. Root cause identified in 5 minutes: SE14 conversion not run after transport. Fix: SE14 Activate and Adjust Database. Recovery time: 20 minutes including verification. Structured diagnosis saved time versus trial-and-error.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Production mein seedha SE14 ya SE11 changes karna without first understanding what went wrong. Diagnosis first, fix second. Unauthorized or wrong fixes in production can cause data loss or longer outages.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Incident diagnosis sequence:
" 1. SM21: System log
"    Filter: Last 2 hours, Error message class
"    Note: exact error time and message

" 2. ST22: ABAP dump analysis
"    Find: dump with production user, recent time
"    Note: dump reason + program name + line number

" 3. SE11: Check table status
SELECT tabname, as4local, as4vers
  FROM dd02l WHERE tabname = 'ZCUSTOM_ORDERS'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt).
" as4local: 'A'=Active, 'M'=Modified (problem!)

" 4. SE14: Check DB vs DDIC discrepancy
"    SE14 → Check → review output → Activate if needed

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle a table activation failure in production after a transport?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Transport ke baad table activation failure typically ek missing dependency ke karan hota hai — transport mein table definition included thi lekin uska domain ya data element nahi. Diagnosis: SE11 mein production mein table open karo aur activate karne ki koshish karo — error message missing object name dikhayega. Verify karo ki missing object production mein nahi hai SE11 mein search karke. Development mein missing object ke liye ek emergency transport create karo, release aur table se pehle correct sequence mein production mein import karo. Missing dependency resolve hone ke baad, SE11 mein table activate karo. Agar issue ek column type change hai table pe data ke saath, SE14 database adjustment chahiye — large tables pe data conversion time ke liye plan karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Table activation failure after transport is typically caused by a missing dependency — the transport included the table definition but not the domain or data element it depends on. Diagnosis: open the table in SE11 in production and attempt to activate — the error message will show the missing object name. Verify the missing object is not in production by searching in SE11. Create an emergency transport for the missing object in development, release and import it to production in correct sequence before the table. After missing dependency is resolved, activate the table in SE11. If the issue is a column type change on a table with data, SE14 database adjustment is needed — plan for data conversion time on large tables.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Go-live incident: table ZCUSTOM_MATERIAL imported but activation failed — 'Domain ZMAT_CLASS not found in system.' Domain was in a separate transport from 3 days ago that was staged but not imported. Emergency import of domain transport (5 minutes). SE11 table activation — success. Entire incident: 18 minutes. Lesson: pre-go-live transport dependency verification prevents this type of incident.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Production mein domain ya data element directly create karna as a quick fix. Never create DDIC objects directly in production — always transport from development. Even for emergencies, transport path must be followed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Diagnose activation failure:
" SE11 → Open ZCUSTOM_MATERIAL → Activate
" Error: 'Domain ZMAT_CLASS does not exist in active version'

" Solution sequence:
" 1. Dev: verify ZMAT_CLASS domain exists and is active
SELECT domname, as4local FROM dd01l
  WHERE domname = 'ZMAT_CLASS' AND as4local = 'A'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_dom).
" 2. Dev: create emergency transport with ZMAT_CLASS only
" 3. Release transport from dev
" 4. STMS: import domain transport to production FIRST
" 5. SE11 production: activate ZCUSTOM_MATERIAL
" 6. Verify: SE11 shows green (active) status

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you safely run SE14 database conversion in production?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Safe SE14 execution in production: Pehle SE14 Check mode run karo — ye dikhata hai actually kiye bina kya hoga. Output mein data loss warnings ke liye check karo, especially agar koi field remove ho raha hai ya column type change ho raha hai. COUNT query ke saath table size estimate karo — large tables (millions of rows) SE14 conversion ke liye minutes se hours le sakte hain. Large tables ke liye, business load low hone pe maintenance window mein execution plan karo. Impact estimate kiye bina large table pe peak hours mein SE14 kabhi mat run karo. Activate aur Adjust Database run karne ke baad, success confirmation ke liye SE14 log check karo — koi errors nahi hone chahiye. Finally, data integrity confirm karne ke liye SE16N row count ko pre-conversion count se match karke verify karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Safe SE14 execution in production: First run SE14 Check mode — this shows what will happen without actually doing it. Check output for data loss warnings, especially if a field is being removed or a column type changed. Estimate table size with a COUNT query — large tables (millions of rows) may take minutes to hours for SE14 conversion. For large tables, plan execution during a maintenance window when business load is low. Never run SE14 on a large table during peak hours without estimating impact. After running Activate and Adjust Database, check the SE14 log for success confirmation — no errors should be present. Finally, verify SE16N row count matches pre-conversion count to confirm data integrity.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production table ZCUSTOM_ORDERS had 2.3 million rows. New field added in transport, SE14 needed. Estimated SE14 time: 2.3M / 3000 rows/sec ≈ 13 minutes. Planned maintenance window Saturday 2 AM. SE14 Check: no data loss warnings (field addition, not deletion). SE14 Adjust: ran 11 minutes. Log: success, no errors. SE16N before: 2,300,847 rows. After: 2,300,847 rows. Data intact. Users started Monday with zero issues.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SE14 Activate and Adjust karna without Check first. Check step shows if any data will be lost or truncated — critical review. Also galti: large table pe SE14 peak hours mein chalana without maintenance window — conversion time mein table may be slow or locked.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Pre-SE14 preparation:
" Step 1: Count rows to estimate conversion time
SELECT COUNT( * ) AS total
  FROM zcustom_orders
  INTO @DATA(lv_total).
WRITE: / 'Total rows:', lv_total.
" Estimate: lv_total / 3000 = approximate seconds

" Step 2: SE14 in production
" Transaction SE14
" Object type: Database Tables
" Table name: ZCUSTOM_ORDERS
" Action 1: Check → review output, no data loss warning
" Action 2: Activate and Adjust Database → run in background
"            (background for large tables)

" Step 3: Post-conversion verification
SELECT COUNT( * ) FROM zcustom_orders INTO @DATA(lv_after).
IF lv_after <> lv_total.
  WRITE: / 'WARNING: Row count mismatch!'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle a production incident where domain fixed values are causing validation failures?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Domain fixed value validation failure tab hota hai jab koi value domain definition mein missing hai — ya to kabhi add nahi hui, ya transport incomplete tha. Diagnosis: SE11 production mein, domain open karo, Fixed Values tab check karo — required value absent hai. Fix straightforward hai: development mein, missing fixed value ko domain mein add karo, activate karo, aur urgent transport create karo. Domain transports small hote hain aur quickly import hote hain. Production mein import aur activation ke baad, validation immediately kaam karti hai — koi system restart nahi chahiye. Previously failing value enter karke verify karo — ab accept honi chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A domain fixed value validation failure occurs when a value is missing from the domain definition — either never added, or the transport was incomplete. Diagnosis: SE11 in production, open the domain, check Fixed Values tab — the required value is absent. The fix is straightforward: in development, add the missing fixed value to the domain, activate, and create an urgent transport. Domain transports are small and import quickly. After import and activation in production, the validation immediately works — no system restart required. Verify by attempting to enter the previously failing value — it should now be accepted.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Incident: New business process introduced 'PEND' status for pending approval. Domain transport was released from development but stopped at QA for approval — never reached production. Business started the process in production — validation failure for PEND everywhere. Emergency: fast-tracked transport approval, imported to production in 45 minutes of incident report. Validation worked. Post-incident: implemented stricter change management checklist for domain changes.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Production mein directly SE11 mein domain value add karna as a shortcut. This creates an object in production that does not exist in development or QA — future transports can overwrite it. Always fix in development and transport.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Diagnose domain fixed value issue:
" SE11 production → Domain ZORDER_STATUS → Fixed Values
" Missing: PEND (Pending Approval)

" Fix in development:
" SE11 Dev → Domain ZORDER_STATUS → Fixed Values → Add PEND
" Description: Pending Approval
" Activate domain in development
" Create transport request with ZORDER_STATUS domain
" Release from development → QA approval → Production

" Verify after import:
SELECT domvalue_l, ddtext
  FROM dd07t
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_values)
  WHERE domname  = 'ZORDER_STATUS'
    AND ddlanguage = 'E'
    AND as4local   = 'A'.
" PEND should now appear in results

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle a production incident where a batch job failed due to a lock not being released?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab ek batch job mid-execution mein bina apne locks release kiye fail hota hai, SM12 stale lock entries dikhata hai — job ka user name, ek old timestamp, aur lock object name. Kyunki job ab run nahi ho raha, ye orphan locks hain jo session timeout tak self-release nahi honge. Fix: SM12, lock object ya user se filter karo, stale entries select karo, aur delete karo. Records deletion ke baad immediately accessible ho jaate hain. Phir batch job failure ko separately analyze karo — program mein root cause fix karo. CLEANUP ya FINALLY block mein DEQUEUE_ALL add karo future stale locks prevent karne ke liye even jab job fail ho.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When a batch job fails mid-execution without releasing its locks, SM12 shows the stale lock entries — the job's user name, an old timestamp, and the lock object name. Since the job is no longer running, these are orphan locks that will not self-release until session timeout. Fix: SM12, filter by lock object or user, select the stale entries, and delete them. Records become immediately accessible after deletion. Then analyze the batch job failure separately — fix the root cause in the program. Add DEQUEUE_ALL in a CLEANUP or FINALLY block to prevent future stale locks even when the job fails.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Monday morning incident: weekend batch job ZBATCH_CONFIG_SYNC failed at 3 AM Saturday. By Monday, 47 config records were locked — all with batch job user BATCH_USER, timestamp from Saturday. SM12: deleted 47 lock entries in 2 minutes. Users immediately accessed records. Root cause: batch job had unhandled exception — no DEQUEUE in error path. Fix deployed: added TRY-CATCH with DEQUEUE_ALL in CLEANUP block. Next weekend job ran successfully, no stale locks.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Stale locks ke liye ABAP program likhna jo directly ENQT table se records delete kare. Never modify lock table directly. SM12 is the correct and safe interface for lock administration.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Identify stale locks from batch job:
" SM12 filter: User = BATCH_USER, Date < today
" Shows: ENQUEUE_EZZCUSTOM_CONFIG entries from Saturday
" Action: Select all → Delete → Locks released

" Root cause fix in batch program:
TRY.
  " Lock records
  CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZZCFG_PLANT' ...
  " Process data
  PERFORM process_config_data.
  " Normal DEQUEUE
  CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_EZZCFG_PLANT' ...
CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_error).
  " Log error
  MESSAGE lx_error TYPE 'E' INTO DATA(lv_msg).
CLEANUP.
  " ALWAYS release all locks on any exit
  CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_ALL'.
ENDTRY.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you communicate during a Data Dictionary production incident?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Production incident communication teen phases follow karta hai. Immediate notification: jaise hi issue confirm ho, team lead, project manager, aur affected users ko minutes mein notify karo — hours mein nahi. State karo: kya fail ho raha hai, kab se, kitne users affected, aur ki investigation underway hai. Investigation ke during: status updates har 15-30 minutes mein even agar abhi koi fix nahi — 'still investigating, root cause X hai, Y minutes mein expected fix.' Resolution announcement: 'Issue HH:MM pe resolved, please retry.' Resolution ke baad: root cause analysis document — events ki timeline, root cause, applied fix, recurrence prevent karne ke steps. Management ke liye: business impact language — SE14, DDIC jaise technical terms avoid karo; bolo 'system update ko database adjustment chahiye thi jo ab complete hai.'
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Production incident communication follows three phases. Immediate notification: as soon as the issue is confirmed, notify team lead, project manager, and affected users within minutes — not hours. State: what is failing, since when, how many users affected, and that investigation is underway. During investigation: status updates every 15-30 minutes even if no fix yet — 'still investigating, root cause is X, expected fix in Y minutes.' Resolution announcement: 'Issue resolved at HH:MM, please retry.' After resolution: root cause analysis document — timeline of events, root cause, fix applied, steps to prevent recurrence. For management: business impact language — avoid technical terms like SE14, DDIC; say 'the system update required a database adjustment which is now complete.'
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Incident communication example: 9 AM alert — 50 users cannot process orders. Immediately: 'Order processing system issue reported, investigating — avoid using ZCUSTOM_ORDER_MAINT until further notice.' 9:15 AM: 'Root cause identified: database not updated after weekend transport. Fix in progress.' 9:28 AM: 'Issue resolved — order processing restored. All pending orders can now be processed.' 9:45 AM: root cause document sent to team. Entire incident 28 minutes — communication kept business calm.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Incident ke dauraan technical team ke beech sirf technical communication karna aur business stakeholders ko update nahi karna. Business unaware rahte hain, panic hoti hai. Jab resolution aata hai unhe reliefmessage bhi nahi milta. Always keep business stakeholders informed.

followupAnswerBank.question: What tools do you use for Data Dictionary production monitoring and incident prevention?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Data Dictionary health ke liye proactive monitoring tools: SM21 system log daily check hona chahiye ya automated — DDIC activation errors yahan user impact se pehle appear hote hain. SM12 periodic review stale locks identify karta hai jo users ne abhi tak report nahi kiye. STMS transport monitoring ensure karta hai sab expected transports import hue hain aur koi backlog nahi hai. DD02L pe query karke as4local 'A' ke barabar na hone wale objects identify karne wala ek custom ABAP report system ke across inactive DDIC objects identify karta hai. SE14 Check ek post-transport standard procedure ke roop mein — koi bhi DDIC transport import karne ke baad hamesha SE14 Check run karo verify karne ke liye ki database synchronized hai. Kuch organizations automated post-transport scripts implement karte hain jo activation status check karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Proactive monitoring tools for Data Dictionary health: SM21 system log should be checked daily or automated — DDIC activation errors appear here before user impact. SM12 periodic review identifies stale locks that users have not yet reported. STMS transport monitoring ensures all expected transports have been imported and no backlog exists. A custom ABAP report querying DD02L for objects with as4local not equal to 'A' identifies inactive DDIC objects across the system. SE14 Check as a post-transport standard procedure — always run SE14 Check after importing any DDIC transport to verify database is synchronized. Some organizations implement automated post-transport scripts that check activation status.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Proactive measure implemented: custom Basis report that runs nightly, queries DD02L for Z* tables with as4local <> 'A' (inactive), emails results to development team. In first month: caught 3 tables that were transported but SE14 not run — fixed before business noticed. Zero DDIC-related production incidents for that quarter after implementing the monitoring.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Reactive approach — wait for users to report issues. Production DDIC monitoring should be proactive. A simple DD02L query scheduled nightly catches problems before they become incidents. Minimal effort, significant prevention.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Proactive monitoring: find inactive custom DDIC objects
SELECT tabname, tabclass, as4local, as4date, as4time
  FROM dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_inactive)
  WHERE tabname   LIKE 'Z%'
    AND as4local <> 'A'   " Not active
    AND tabclass  = 'TRANSP'.  " Transparent tables only

IF lt_inactive IS NOT INITIAL.
  " Send alert email to development team
  " Each inactive table is a potential production risk
  LOOP AT lt_inactive INTO DATA(ls_obj).
    WRITE: / ls_obj-tabname, ls_obj-as4local,
             ls_obj-as4date, ls_obj-as4time.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.
" Schedule as background job, daily 06:00 AM

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you do a post-incident review for a Data Dictionary production issue?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Post-incident review 5 Whys approach follow karta hai true root cause tak pahuchne ke liye. Timeline: reconstruct karo exactly kya hua aur kab — transport time X pe import hua, issue time Y pe start hua, time Z pe report hua. Root cause: issue kyun hua — transport import ke baad SE14 nahi chala. SE14 kyun nahi chala — post-transport SE14 verification ke liye koi checklist item nahi tha. Checklist item kyun nahi tha — post-transport procedure informal tha. Is analysis se action items: SE14 Check ko mandatory step ke roop mein include karke formal post-transport checklist create karo, ownership assign karo, deadline define karo. Severity, impact, resolution time, aur prevention measures ke saath incident tracker mein document karo. Team meeting mein learnings share karo — doosron dwara repeat prevent karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Post-incident review follows the 5 Whys approach to reach true root cause. Timeline: reconstruct exactly what happened and when — transport imported at time X, issue started at time Y, reported at time Z. Root cause: why did the issue occur — SE14 was not run after transport import. Why was SE14 not run — no checklist item for post-transport SE14 verification. Why was there no checklist item — post-transport procedure was informal. Action items from this analysis: create formal post-transport checklist including SE14 Check as mandatory step, assign ownership, define deadline. Document in incident tracker with severity, impact, resolution time, and prevention measures. Share learnings in team meeting — prevents repeat by others.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Post-incident: 5 Whys on SE14 not run. Why 1: SE14 not run after transport. Why 2: no standard checklist. Why 3: informal knowledge only. Why 4: team assumed previous developer handled it. Why 5: no ownership defined. Prevention: formal post-transport checklist created, Basis team owns execution, developer confirms SE14 not needed or runs it. Zero repeat incidents for DDIC in next 6 months.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Post-incident review sirf ek informal team chat karna aur koi action items document nahi karna. Without written action items with owners and deadlines, same incident repeats in 3 months. Always document, assign, track.
